This is regarding the question arose in sympy mailing-list, 
```
from sympy.diffgeom import WedgeProduct
from sympy.diffgeom.rn import R2_r

e_x,e_y = R2_r.base_vectors()
dx,dy = R2_r.base_oneforms()
print(WedgeProduct(dx, dy)(e_x, e_y, 2*e_x))
```

This outputs -2 as the answer. Clarify the solution.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26401/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26401/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2032414296","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26401#issuecomment-2032414296","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26401","id":2032414296,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs55JC5Y","user":{"login":"nullHawk","id":83297944,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMjk3OTQ0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/83297944?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk","html_url":"https://github.com/nullHawk","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nullHawk/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-02T15:42:50Z","updated_at":"2024-04-02T15:42:50Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> print(WedgeProduct(dx, dy)(e_x, e_y, 2*e_x))

This expression calculates the wedge product of the base one-forms `dx` and `dy`, and then evaluates the resulting multivector on the vectors `e_x`, `e_y`, and `2*e_x`. However, this expression doesn't directly represent `e_x^e_y^(2*e_x)`. As far as I know, it will be represented as `WedgeProduct(e_x, e_y, 2 * e_x)`, but when I tried running it, SymPy doesn't simplify this
